_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic and treatment framework anchored to the international EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI 2022 chronic urticaria guideline (Zuberbier et al. Allergy 2022, PMID 34324010) and the 2024 AAAAI Practice Parameter Update (Bernstein et al.). Omalizumab evidence from ASTERIA I/II and GLACIAL trials (Maurer NEJM 2013, PMID 23432142). Epidemiology per Fricke et al. Allergy 2020 (PMID 31729045). Autologous serum skin test and autoimmune urticaria from Konstantinou et al. Allergy 2013 (PMID 23445307). UAS7 validation per Mathias et al. 2010.

h1Title: Chronic Urticaria: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic urticaria is the recurrent appearance of itchy, raised, pale-centered wheals with a red flare and/or deep tissue swelling (angioedema) that persist or recur for more than six weeks. Individual lesions resolve within 24 hours without bruising, but new ones continually appear in different locations. Global prevalence is approximately 1% of the population at any time, and lifetime prevalence reaches 5% (Fricke 2020), with women affected twice as often as men and peak onset between ages 20 and 40. About two-thirds of cases are chronic spontaneous urticaria with no identifiable external trigger; one-third are chronic inducible forms triggered by cold, pressure, heat, exercise, sunlight, water, or vibration. Quality of life impact is profound — chronic urticaria scores worse on standardized quality-of-life instruments than coronary artery disease in many studies. First-line treatment is a second-generation H1 antihistamine, escalated up to four times the standard dose; omalizumab and other biologics achieve well-controlled disease in 65-85% of patients refractory to antihistamines.

keyStats.prevalence: Point prevalence approximately 1% of the population; lifetime prevalence approximately 5% (Fricke et al. Allergy 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 2x more often than men; peak onset ages 20-40 years; uncommon in young children but well-documented in pediatric patients
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of onset 35-40 years; chronic inducible forms more common in younger adults
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 75-80 million people worldwide live with chronic urticaria at any given time; mean disease duration 1-5 years, with 20% having symptoms beyond 5 years

definition: Chronic urticaria (ICD-10: L50.1 idiopathic, L50.8 other) is defined by the international EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI 2022 guideline as the appearance of wheals (hives), angioedema, or both for more than six weeks. The condition is classified into two main subtypes: chronic spontaneous urticaria (formerly chronic idiopathic urticaria), where wheals appear without an identifiable trigger; and chronic inducible urticaria, where a specific physical or environmental trigger reproducibly elicits lesions (cold, heat, pressure, exercise, vibration, sunlight, water, cholinergic stimulation). Both subtypes can coexist in the same patient. Pathophysiologically, mast-cell activation and degranulation in the skin release histamine, leukotrienes, and other mediators causing the characteristic wheal-and-flare reaction; recent evidence implicates type IIb autoimmune mechanisms (IgG against IgE or the high-affinity IgE receptor FcεRI) in roughly 40-50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria. The disease is managed primarily by allergy and immunology specialists and dermatologists, with a stepped pharmacologic approach. Despite the name, chronic urticaria is rarely associated with classical IgE-mediated food or drug allergy in adults; routine allergy testing is not recommended for chronic spontaneous urticaria without a clear history of acute reactions to specific triggers.

typesClassification.description: Wheals, angioedema, or both occurring without identifiable external trigger for more than six weeks. Accounts for 60-75% of chronic urticaria. Up to 50% involves autoimmune mechanisms with IgG autoantibodies. Subdivided into autoimmune (type IIb) and non-autoimmune forms by autologous serum skin test and basophil activation testing.

The most common chronic inducible urticaria. Linear wheals form along lines of firm stroking or scratching of the skin within 5 minutes. Affects 2-5% of the general population. Diagnosed by reproducing the wheal with a calibrated dermographometer or firm stroking pressure.

Wheals and sometimes systemic reactions including anaphylaxis develop on skin exposed to cold air, water, or surfaces. Diagnosed with the ice cube test. Swimming in cold water is the most dangerous exposure; patient education and an epinephrine auto-injector are standard.

Small (1-3 mm) pinpoint wheals with surrounding flare appear during a rise in core body temperature from exercise, hot showers, or emotional stress. More common in young adults. Often confused with exercise-induced anaphylaxis, which involves food cofactors.

typesClassification.type: Delayed pressure urticaria, solar urticaria, aquagenic urticaria, vibratory urticaria
typesClassification.description: Less common inducible urticarias triggered by sustained pressure (4-8 hours after stimulus), ultraviolet or visible light, water contact at any temperature, and vibration respectively. Confirmed with standardized provocation testing in specialist clinics.

primarySymptoms: Itchy, raised, pale-centered wheals (hives) with surrounding redness, varying from a few millimeters to many centimeters, appearing on any part of the body.
Individual wheals fade within 24 hours without bruising, scarring, or pigmentation change, but new ones continually arise so the rash appears persistent.
Angioedema — deep, painful swelling of lips, eyelids, hands, feet, or genitals — accompanies wheals in 30-50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria cases and resolves over 48-72 hours.
Severe pruritus that worsens at night, often interferes with sleep, and prompts scratching; the itch is typically described as burning or stinging rather than purely itchy.
Skin tingling or burning that may precede visible wheals by minutes.
Wheals reproducibly triggered by cold, pressure, heat, sunlight, water, or exercise in chronic inducible urticaria.
Cyclical or unpredictable disease activity with day-to-day variability and frequent flares related to infection, NSAIDs, alcohol, stress, or menstruation.

Marked impairment of sleep, mood, work productivity, and social function — Chronic Urticaria Quality of Life Questionnaire (CU-Q2oL) scores often equal or exceed those in moderate-to-severe psoriasis.
Joint pains and fatigue in a subset of patients, especially those with autoimmune subtype.
Systemic symptoms (lightheadedness, abdominal pain) in cold urticaria and cholinergic urticaria with widespread skin involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: Hives recurring on most days for 2-4 weeks without an identifiable allergen
New or recurrent angioedema episodes without urticaria
Hives appearing reproducibly after cold, pressure, heat, or sun exposure
Sleep disruption from nocturnal itch lasting more than 4 weeks
Hives accompanied by joint pains, fevers, or weight loss — prompts evaluation for urticarial vasculitis or autoinflammatory syndromes
emergencySigns: Tongue, lip, or throat swelling causing difficulty breathing, drooling, or voice change — call emergency services and administer epinephrine if available
Wheezing, stridor, or hypotension during a hives flare suggests systemic anaphylaxis (especially in cold urticaria after swimming, or food cofactor exposure)
Hives that do not fade within 24 hours, are painful rather than itchy, leave bruising, or are accompanied by fever, joint pain, or weight loss — exclude urticarial vasculitis with biopsy
Hives in the setting of new fever, joint swelling, or rash after a recent illness or new medication — evaluate for serum sickness or autoimmune disease
First episode of generalized urticaria after a specific food, insect sting, or medication exposure — exclude acute IgE-mediated anaphylaxis

causes.description: In 30-50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria, IgG autoantibodies against IgE or the high-affinity IgE receptor (FcεRI) directly activate mast cells. Identified by positive autologous serum skin test, basophil activation testing, or elevated anti-thyroid peroxidase or anti-FcεRI antibodies. This subtype responds well to omalizumab but may require longer treatment and higher doses.

causes.cause: Mast cell activation through type I autoallergy (IgE against autoantigens)
causes.description: Patients have IgE antibodies against their own autoantigens (interleukin-24, thyroid peroxidase, double-stranded DNA, others). These IgE molecules bind FcεRI on skin mast cells and trigger degranulation upon antigen contact. Often coexists with type IIb autoimmunity and responds to omalizumab.

Helicobacter pylori, hepatitis B and C, parasitic infections (Anisakis, Strongyloides, Toxocara), and chronic dental or sinus infections have been associated with chronic urticaria. Treatment of identified infection helps approximately 20-40% of patients in observational studies, though high-quality evidence is limited.

causes.cause: Physical and environmental triggers (chronic inducible urticaria)
causes.description: Cold, heat, ultraviolet or visible light, sustained pressure, vibration, water contact, exercise, and cholinergic stimuli reproducibly trigger wheals. The wheals occur because the physical stimulus directly activates skin mast cells. Often coexists with chronic spontaneous urticaria.

Aspirin, other NSAIDs, opioids, and ACE inhibitors (especially for angioedema) commonly aggravate chronic urticaria without being the primary cause. Pseudoallergens (food additives, salicylates) worsen disease in a subset of patients but rarely act as a single cause. Alcohol is a common flare trigger.

causes.cause: Autoimmune thyroid disease and other autoimmune comorbidities
causes.description: Anti-thyroid antibodies are present in 12-30% of chronic spontaneous urticaria patients, two to three times higher than the general population. Thyroid disease itself is not the cause of urticaria but is a marker of shared autoimmune susceptibility. Other associations include vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and celiac disease.

riskFactors.description: Women have approximately twice the risk of men, possibly related to estrogen effects on mast cells and higher autoimmunity rates in women. Disease activity may fluctuate with menstrual cycle and pregnancy.
Peak incidence in young to middle-aged adults; less common but well-documented in children and older adults.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: Hashimoto's thyroiditis, vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and celiac disease are associated with 2-3x higher chronic urticaria risk and suggest shared autoimmune susceptibility.

Several observational studies link H. pylori eradication to urticaria remission in 20-40% of infected patients. International guidelines suggest H. pylori testing in chronic urticaria when there is access to test-and-treat infrastructure.
Aspirin and non-selective NSAIDs worsen wheals in 20-30% of chronic urticaria patients. Switching to COX-2-selective agents or alternative analgesics often improves disease control.
Personal or family history of atopic disease modestly increases chronic urticaria risk; the relationship is weaker than for acute urticaria.

Acute viral or bacterial infections precede the onset of chronic urticaria in a subset of patients, suggesting an infectious trigger of mast-cell autoimmunity. COVID-19 has been linked to new-onset chronic urticaria in case series.

affectedDemographics: Adults of working age: peak prevalence ages 20-40, lower in young children and older adults
Women: twice as commonly affected as men in all studied populations
Patients with autoimmune disease: 2-3x higher prevalence in those with autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo, or type 1 diabetes
Higher reported prevalence in industrialized countries (1-1.5%) than rural settings, possibly reflecting both case ascertainment and environmental factors
Pediatric chronic urticaria: estimated 0.5-1% in children; usually less severe and more often remitting than adult disease
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the characteristic appearance of wheals and/or angioedema for more than six weeks. Routine extensive laboratory testing is not recommended for typical chronic spontaneous urticaria. The international 2022 guideline recommends a minimum workup of complete blood count and C-reactive protein or erythrocyte sedimentation rate for all patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria, with additional targeted tests guided by history (e.g., thyroid function and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody if autoimmunity is suspected, vitamin D, infection screening for H. pylori or chronic hepatitis where clinically warranted). Disease activity is measured using the Urticaria Activity Score over 7 days (UAS7), which combines daily wheal count (0-3) and itch intensity (0-3) for a total of 0-42; clinically meaningful improvement requires a UAS7 reduction of at least 11 points. Angioedema-only disease is assessed by the Angioedema Activity Score (AAS). The Urticaria Control Test (UCT) and CU-Q2oL track patient-reported control and quality of life. Chronic inducible urticarias require provocation testing: ice cube test for cold urticaria, calibrated dermographometer for symptomatic dermographism, pulse-controlled ergometer for cholinergic urticaria, and standardized weight test for delayed pressure urticaria. Skin biopsy is reserved for cases where individual wheals persist beyond 24 hours, leave bruising, or are painful — to exclude urticarial vasculitis. Allergy skin prick and specific IgE testing are not routinely indicated for chronic spontaneous urticaria.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the duration (>6 weeks), presence of wheals and/or angioedema, individual wheal lifespan (<24 hours), and search for triggers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A focused interview covering wheal pattern, triggers, prior medications, infections, family history, and impact on sleep and quality of life. Examination during a flare confirms typical morphology and excludes mimics. No special preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies disease activity and treatment response over time using a validated daily diary
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Daily self-report of wheal count and itch severity each evening for one week. Score 0-42 (higher = worse). Used at baseline and every 4-12 weeks during therapy to guide step-up or step-down decisions.

diagnosticTests.test: Basic laboratory workup (CBC with differential, CRP or ESR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for systemic inflammation, eosinophilia (parasitic disease, drug hypersensitivity), or features suggesting urticarial vasculitis or autoinflammatory syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw with results in 24-48 hours. Elevated CRP/ESR or abnormal blood cell counts trigger further targeted testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Provocation testing for chronic inducible urticaria
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms specific inducible subtypes (cold, pressure, heat, dermographism, cholinergic, solar, aquagenic, vibratory) and provides a quantitative threshold to monitor
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized in-clinic provocation: ice cube on forearm for 5 minutes (cold), brisk exercise on ergometer (cholinergic), stroke with dermographometer (dermographism), weighted strap (pressure). Wheal appearance within minutes confirms diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4, anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies autoimmune thyroid disease present in 12-30% of CSU patients; supports autoimmune subtype identification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results in 1-3 days. Abnormal thyroid function or positive anti-TPO antibodies may warrant endocrinology referral and influence biologic therapy choice.

diagnosticTests.test: Autologous serum skin test (ASST) or basophil activation test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects functional autoantibodies that activate basophils, identifying type IIb autoimmune urticaria
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Available in specialist clinics. ASST: patient's own serum injected intradermally, wheal at 30 minutes is positive. Basophil tests use flow cytometry of donor or patient basophils. Result guides prognosis and biologic choice.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes urticarial vasculitis when wheals last >24 hours, are painful, or leave bruising or pigmentation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient punch biopsy of a fresh wheal under local anesthesia. Histology with leukocytoclastic infiltrate and fibrinoid necrosis confirms urticarial vasculitis, which requires different treatment (often immunosuppression rather than antihistamines).

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows the international 2022 EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI guideline stepped algorithm. Step 1: a second-generation H1-antihistamine at standard dose (cetirizine 10 mg, levocetirizine 5 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, loratadine 10 mg, desloratadine 5 mg, bilastine 20 mg, rupatadine 10 mg) once daily. Step 2: increase the same antihistamine up to four times the standard dose (e.g., cetirizine 40 mg daily) if symptoms persist after 2-4 weeks; this is off-label in many countries but specifically recommended by the international guideline. Step 3: add omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks if symptoms persist on high-dose antihistamine for 4 weeks. Omalizumab achieves complete or near-complete control in 65-85% of patients within 12 weeks (ASTERIA I, ASTERIA II, GLACIAL trials). Step 4: increase omalizumab dose or frequency, switch to cyclosporine 2.5-5 mg/kg/day (for omalizumab-refractory cases), or refer for newer biologics including ligelizumab (anti-IgE), dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα), or remibrutinib (BTK inhibitor). Short courses of oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 20-40 mg for 3-10 days) are used only for severe flares, not as maintenance. First-generation sedating antihistamines (hydroxyzine, diphenhydramine) are not recommended as first-line because of cognitive side effects. Patient education on disease unpredictability, trigger avoidance where applicable, and quality-of-life monitoring is central. Most cases of chronic spontaneous urticaria remit spontaneously within 2-5 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation H1-antihistamines (cetirizine, levocetirizine, fexofenadine, bilastine, rupatadine, desloratadine, loratadine)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy at standard once-daily doses. Bilastine and rupatadine have additional anti-inflammatory properties. Avoid sedating first-generation antihistamines except very short courses for severe nocturnal itch.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard doses achieve complete control in 30-45% of patients; partial control in another 30%

medicalTreatments.name: Up-dosed second-generation H1-antihistamines (up to 4x standard dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Stepped escalation per international guideline. Cetirizine 40 mg, levocetirizine 20 mg, fexofenadine 720 mg daily. Off-label in many countries but specifically recommended by EAACI/GA²LEN.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Additional 20-30% of patients achieve control; combined with standard dosing, approximately 50-65% reach acceptable disease control

medicalTreatments.name: Omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IgE monoclonal antibody. First-line biologic when antihistamines fail. Approved by FDA and EMA for chronic spontaneous urticaria. Dose can be increased to 600 mg or shortened to 2-week intervals in refractory cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 65-85% achieve well-controlled disease (UAS7 ≤ 6) within 12 weeks; 35-45% achieve complete remission. Effects typically appear within 1-4 weeks of first dose

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine 2.5-5 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line biologic-sparing option for omalizumab-refractory chronic spontaneous urticaria. Requires monitoring of blood pressure and renal function. Course duration typically 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-70% response rate in omalizumab-refractory patients; relapse common after stopping

medicalTreatments.name: H2-antihistamines (famotidine, cimetidine) as adjuncts
medicalTreatments.description: Limited additional benefit over H1-antihistamine monotherapy in modern trials; sometimes added when H1 step-up has been maximized. International guideline no longer recommends as routine adjunct.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small additional response (10-15%) in some patients; not as effective as omalizumab

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 20-40 mg for 3-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe acute flares of chronic urticaria. Not used as maintenance therapy because of adverse effects with chronic exposure. Often tapered as antihistamine or omalizumab dose is optimized.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptom control within 24-72 hours; relapse common after course completion if maintenance therapy is not optimized

medicalTreatments.name: Newer biologics (ligelizumab, dupilumab, remibrutinib)
medicalTreatments.description: Emerging therapies for omalizumab-refractory chronic urticaria. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) approved in some jurisdictions for refractory CSU; remibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) in late-phase trials with promising results.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-75% response in selected refractory patients in phase 3 trials; availability varies by country

alternativeTreatments.name: Pseudoallergen-low diet (4 weeks trial)
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoids food additives, salicylates, biogenic amines, and natural aromatics. Modest benefit in a 30-40% subset with confirmed pseudoallergen reactivity in DBPCFC trials. Not recommended as first-line because of low yield and significant lifestyle burden.

alternativeTreatments.name: Stress-reduction and mindfulness-based interventions
alternativeTreatments.description: Chronic urticaria flares are exacerbated by psychological stress in 30-40% of patients. Mindfulness-based stress reduction and CBT improve quality-of-life scores and may reduce flare frequency in small trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with worse chronic urticaria activity. Supplementation to normal serum 25-OH vitamin D (≥30 ng/mL) modestly improves UAS7 in randomized trials; cheap and well tolerated as an adjunct.

whySeeSpecialist: Allergy and immunology specialists or dermatologists with urticaria experience can confirm the diagnosis, exclude urticarial vasculitis and angioedema mimics, perform provocation testing for inducible forms, and prescribe biologic therapy. Specialist referral is recommended when symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks of high-dose antihistamines, when angioedema is prominent, when individual wheals last more than 24 hours, or when systemic features are present.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in allergy and immunology (ABMS in the US, equivalent international boards) or dermatology with documented urticaria expertise. Specialized urticaria centers (UCARE — Urticaria Centers of Reference and Excellence) accredited by GA²LEN provide structured care and access to clinical trials. Confirm experience with omalizumab and inducible urticaria provocation testing.
hospitalCriteria: Allergy and immunology service with biologic infusion capability
Provocation testing facility (TempTest, FricTest, calibrated dermographometer)
Dermatology service for biopsy and differential diagnosis
Access to omalizumab and second-line biologic therapies
keyFacilities: Allergy/immunology outpatient clinics with biologic infusion
Dermatology departments with provocation testing capability
Tertiary autoimmunity centers for complex refractory cases
preventionStrategies: Avoid known individual triggers (specific NSAIDs, alcohol, identified physical stimuli) once they are clearly correlated with flares.
Address treatable comorbidities: optimize thyroid replacement, treat documented Helicobacter pylori, manage chronic infections.
Maintain a UAS7 diary to identify pattern and triggers and to guide therapy adjustments.
Use sun protection in solar urticaria; layer warm clothing and avoid swimming in cold water in cold urticaria; carry an epinephrine auto-injector if systemic reactions have occurred.
Limit aspirin and non-selective NSAIDs in patients with documented aggravation; switch to acetaminophen or COX-2-selective agents under medical guidance.
Manage stress and sleep deprivation, which are frequent flare triggers, through structured behavioral interventions.
lifestyleModifications: Take antihistamine doses consistently at the same time each day; missed doses are a common cause of apparent treatment failure.
Keep a daily symptom diary for at least 4 weeks at diagnosis and during treatment changes.
Identify and minimize trigger exposure where applicable (cold water, sustained pressure, heat, sun).
Maintain low-stress sleep environment with cool bedroom and breathable fabric to reduce nocturnal itch.
Use fragrance-free, soap-free skin cleansers and lukewarm rather than hot showers.
Carry an emergency action plan and adrenaline auto-injector if cold urticaria or anaphylactic features are documented.
Update treating clinician immediately if new medications, infections, or pregnancy occur, as therapy may need adjustment.

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating pattern, which is anti-inflammatory and well tolerated
Vitamin D-rich foods (fatty fish, fortified dairy, eggs) and supplementation if deficiency confirmed
Plenty of fresh fruit and vegetables to support general immune balance
Adequate hydration; cool fluids may reduce cholinergic urticaria flares
avoid: Alcohol — a common flare trigger; abstain during active disease
Suspected aggravators identified on personal symptom diary (e.g., specific food additives, aged cheese, fermented products)
Aspirin and non-selective NSAIDs if previously documented to flare urticaria
Very hot foods or beverages and spicy meals in cholinergic urticaria — they raise core body temperature

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise improves overall wellbeing and is safe for most patients with chronic urticaria. In cholinergic urticaria, take a second-generation antihistamine 30-60 minutes before exercise, warm up gradually, and stop if widespread wheals develop. In cold urticaria, avoid swimming alone in cold water — sudden generalized cold exposure can cause systemic reactions and drowning. Inform exercise partners and instructors about the condition and emergency plan.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antihistamine or biologic on schedule; consistency outperforms intermittent dosing.
Record UAS7 (wheal count and itch severity) each evening during active disease.
Apply cool compresses or menthol/calamine lotions for localized symptom relief.
Avoid hot showers and spicy food if cholinergic features are present.
Photograph wheals to share with clinician between visits; aids assessment of severity and unusual features.
Review medication list at every visit for potential aggravators (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, opioids).
prognosis: Outlook is favorable: roughly 50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria patients achieve remission within 1 year, and 80% within 5 years. Disease duration is longer in those with concurrent angioedema, autoimmune subtype, severe baseline activity, or chronic inducible features (especially cold urticaria, which often persists 5-10 years). Modern stepped therapy achieves disease control in 85-95% of patients overall: 50-65% on high-dose antihistamines, an additional 20-30% on omalizumab, and the remainder with cyclosporine or newer biologics. Quality of life improves substantially even before complete clearance: meaningful UAS7 improvement is reported within 4-12 weeks of starting omalizumab. Mortality from chronic urticaria is essentially nil; complications are dominated by mental health and sleep impacts. Pediatric chronic urticaria has a higher spontaneous remission rate than adult disease, often within 1-2 years.
recoveryTimeline: Standard-dose antihistamines may reduce symptoms within hours; full effect within 1-2 weeks. High-dose antihistamines: response evaluated at 2-4 weeks. Omalizumab: first response often within 1-4 weeks; full effect by 12 weeks. Cyclosporine: response within 4-8 weeks. Spontaneous remission of chronic spontaneous urticaria typically occurs within 1-5 years; chronic inducible forms (especially cold urticaria) may last longer.
complications: Significant sleep disruption, with cumulative impact on cognition, mood, and work productivity
Anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress symptoms — present in 25-40% of patients with poorly controlled disease
Severe angioedema involving the airway in 1-5% of cases, requiring emergency management
Anaphylaxis in cold urticaria (especially with cold water immersion) and cholinergic urticaria with food cofactors
Adverse effects of chronic corticosteroid use (osteoporosis, weight gain, hyperglycemia) when steroid courses are repeated rather than escalating to biologics
Substantial direct and indirect economic burden — averaging more than €3,000 per patient per year in European cost-of-illness studies

supportResources.name: Urticaria Network (UNEV / Centers of Reference and Excellence)
supportResources.description: Authoritative network of accredited urticaria centers with patient resources, validated questionnaires (UAS7, UCT), and registry data.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI) — urticaria patient page
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed patient education on chronic urticaria from the US academy of allergy and immunology.

supportResources.name: Allergy UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity with chronic urticaria information leaflets, helpline, and support community.

supportResources.name: GA²LEN — Global Allergy and Asthma European Network
supportResources.description: International network publishing the joint urticaria treatment guideline and coordinating UCARE accreditation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces persistent, ill-defined, dry, scaly patches with lichenification in flexural areas (elbows, knees, neck) over weeks to months. Chronic urticaria produces transient wheals that resolve within 24 hours without scaling or pigmentation change. Eczema is treated with topical corticosteroids and moisturizers, urticaria with H1-antihistamines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-demarcated red plaques with thick silvery scale, typically on extensor surfaces, scalp, and sacrum. Lesions persist for weeks to months. Chronic urticaria wheals are transient, smooth, lack scale, and resolve within 24 hours. Treatments are completely different.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (Urticarial Vasculitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Urticarial vasculitis associated with lupus produces wheals that persist >24 hours, are painful or burning rather than itchy, leave bruising or pigmentation, and are accompanied by joint pain, fever, or weight loss. Biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis. Treated with steroids and immunosuppression, not antihistamines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic relapsing inflammatory skin disease with itch, dry skin, and characteristic distribution. Lesions persist and evolve to chronic lichenified plaques. Chronic urticaria lacks chronic skin change and produces only transient wheals.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared atopic background and IL-4/IL-13 biology; dupilumab is approved for both and is in trials for refractory chronic urticaria.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Common atopic comorbidity in chronic urticaria; omalizumab is approved for both indications.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroid disease coexists in 12-30% of chronic urticaria; shared autoimmune susceptibility.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic comorbidity that can complicate skin assessment and contribute to itch burden.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential diagnosis for urticarial vasculitis and other rash that lasts longer than 24 hours with systemic features.

faqs.question: What is chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Chronic urticaria is the recurrent appearance of itchy wheals (hives) and/or deep tissue swelling (angioedema) for more than six weeks. Individual wheals resolve within 24 hours but new ones continually appear. Most cases have no identifiable external trigger and are called chronic spontaneous urticaria.

faqs.question: How long does chronic urticaria last?
faqs.answer: About 50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria patients enter remission within one year and 80% within five years. Chronic inducible forms (especially cold urticaria) may last longer. Modern stepped therapy controls symptoms in 85-95% of patients regardless of underlying duration.

faqs.question: What causes chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: In 30-50% of chronic spontaneous urticaria, autoimmune mechanisms involve IgG against IgE or its receptor, or IgE against autoantigens. Other factors include chronic infections, physical triggers in inducible forms, and autoimmune thyroid disease. Most cases are not caused by food or drug allergy.

faqs.question: Is chronic urticaria an allergy?
faqs.answer: Despite the resemblance to allergic reactions, chronic urticaria is rarely caused by classical IgE-mediated allergy to a single food or drug. Routine allergy skin testing is not recommended. The condition is best understood as a mast-cell activation disorder, often with autoimmune drivers.

faqs.question: How is chronic urticaria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: recurrent wheals or angioedema for more than six weeks. Initial workup includes complete blood count and inflammatory markers. Provocation testing confirms inducible forms. Extensive routine allergy testing is not recommended. Disease activity is tracked with the Urticaria Activity Score over 7 days (UAS7).

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Second-generation H1-antihistamines (cetirizine, levocetirizine, fexofenadine, bilastine, rupatadine, desloratadine, loratadine) at standard once-daily dose. If symptoms persist after 2-4 weeks, the dose is increased up to four times the standard amount per international guideline.

faqs.question: What is omalizumab and when is it used?
faqs.answer: Omalizumab is an anti-IgE monoclonal antibody given as a 300 mg subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks. It is used for chronic spontaneous urticaria that has not responded to high-dose second-generation antihistamines for 4 weeks. Omalizumab achieves well-controlled disease in 65-85% of patients within 12 weeks.

faqs.question: Can chronic urticaria be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no curative therapy, but most cases enter spontaneous remission within 1-5 years. Modern treatment achieves disease control in 85-95% of patients, allowing normal activities even before remission. Some patients have a single episode of chronic urticaria in their lifetime; others have recurring courses.

faqs.question: Do I need allergy testing for chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Routine skin prick or specific IgE testing is not recommended for chronic spontaneous urticaria because classical IgE-mediated allergy is rarely the cause. Targeted testing is considered when a specific food, drug, or sting reproducibly triggers wheals or anaphylaxis.

faqs.question: Does diet affect chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: For most patients, diet has limited impact. A subset (30-40%) responds to a pseudoallergen-low diet excluding food additives, salicylates, and biogenic amines, but the diet is restrictive and the benefit modest. Alcohol commonly aggravates flares and is best avoided during active disease.

faqs.question: Is chronic urticaria contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Chronic urticaria is not infectious and cannot be transmitted between people through contact. Although certain infections may trigger or aggravate the disease in some patients, the urticaria itself is a non-contagious immune disorder.

faqs.question: Can stress cause chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Stress does not cause chronic urticaria but is a common flare trigger in 30-40% of patients. Stress reduction techniques and treatment of comorbid anxiety improve quality-of-life scores and may reduce flare frequency. Psychological support is a useful adjunct to medical therapy.

faqs.question: What is angioedema and how does it differ from urticaria?
faqs.answer: Angioedema is deep tissue swelling, most often of lips, eyelids, hands, feet, or genitals. It lasts 48-72 hours and is painful rather than itchy. Urticaria affects the upper layers of skin and produces itchy raised wheals lasting under 24 hours. The two often coexist in chronic urticaria.

faqs.question: Is chronic urticaria associated with autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 12-30% of chronic spontaneous urticaria patients have autoimmune thyroid disease, and there are increased rates of vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Screening for thyroid disease is reasonable at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can chronic urticaria be dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most chronic urticaria is not dangerous, but severe angioedema involving the airway and anaphylaxis (especially in cold urticaria after cold water immersion) can be life-threatening. Patients with these features should carry an adrenaline auto-injector and an emergency action plan.

faqs.question: Can children get chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric chronic urticaria has an estimated prevalence of 0.5-1%. Symptoms are usually less severe than in adults and remit more often within 1-2 years. Treatment follows the same stepped algorithm with weight-adjusted antihistamine doses; omalizumab is approved from age 12 in most countries.

faqs.question: What is dermographism?
faqs.answer: Dermographism (literally 'skin writing') is the most common chronic inducible urticaria. Linear wheals form along lines of firm stroking or scratching within 5 minutes. It affects 2-5% of the general population and is diagnosed by reproducing the wheal with a calibrated dermographometer.

faqs.question: Should chronic urticaria treatment be taken during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes, if symptoms warrant. Loratadine and cetirizine have the most reassuring safety data in pregnancy and are first-line. Omalizumab data in pregnancy are accumulating and the medication is generally considered safe to continue if needed. Decisions are made jointly with the obstetrician and allergist.

faqs.question: Can chronic urticaria come back after treatment stops?
faqs.answer: Yes. After omalizumab is stopped, 30-50% of patients have symptom recurrence within 3-12 months, often controlled by restarting therapy. Spontaneous remission rates are high overall, but recurrent courses are well described. Long-term follow-up by an allergist is helpful.

faqs.question: Are there new treatments for chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ligelizumab (next-generation anti-IgE), dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα), and remibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) are in late-phase trials or approval pathways for refractory chronic spontaneous urticaria. They expand options for patients who do not respond to omalizumab.

faqs.question: Does Helicobacter pylori cause chronic urticaria?
faqs.answer: H. pylori does not directly cause chronic urticaria but is associated with the condition in some patients. Eradication helps approximately 20-40% of infected patients in observational studies. International guidelines suggest testing where infrastructure is available and treating documented infection.

metaTitle: Chronic Urticaria: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Chronic urticaria: itchy wheals or angioedema lasting over 6 weeks. Diagnosis with UAS7, antihistamine and omalizumab treatment, medically reviewed.
